1: The queen of Sheba (malkat-Šəḇāʾ) represents the Sabaean kingdom of southwest Arabia (modern Yemen), the dominant power in the ancient spice trade. Sabaean merchants controlled the overland caravan routes from the Arabian Peninsula to the Levantine coast, trading incense, myrrh, gold, and luxury goods. Saba’s capital, Marib, featured one of the ancient world’s great dams, and Sabaean inscriptions attest an advanced literate culture. Her visit “to test him with riddles” (ḥîḏôt) fits the ANE tradition of wisdom contests between royal courts; Proverbs 1:6 lists ḥîḏāh (riddle) as a wisdom genre. The “name of the LORD” — her stated motivation — signals that Solomon’s wisdom had acquired a specifically theological reputation among the nations, fulfilling the prayer of 1 Kings 8:41-43.
2: Her delegation brings the premier commodities of south Arabian trade: spices (bəśāmîm, aromatic resins), gold, and precious stones. The “very large retinue” and camels underline the distance and scale of the journey. The spice trade from Arabia was economically vital to the entire ancient Near East; the same goods appear in the Exodus tabernacle materials (Exodus 25:7) and in the tribute lists of Assyrian kings. Her coming to Solomon with these gifts frames the encounter as both diplomatic mission and recognition of Israelite supremacy at the height of the empire.
3: Solomon answered all her questions: lōʾ-hāyāh dāḇār nistar min-hammelek ʾăšer lōʾ-higgîd lāh — there was nothing hidden from the king that he could not explain. The verb nāgad (declare, make plain) in the causative describes the sage who illuminates opaque things. The wisdom literature tradition values this capacity — the sage who makes the obscure plain resembles YHWH who reveals mysteries (Daniel 2:22). The queen’s riddle-testing frames Solomon as the exemplary wise king.
4: She sees “all the wisdom of Solomon and the house he had built” — wisdom and architecture are presented as a unified display. The Temple and palace complex was itself a form of enacted wisdom: its proportions, materials, and symbolism expressed cosmic order. ANE royal inscriptions regularly link wisdom to building — Gudea of Lagash’s cylinder inscriptions and Egyptian royal didactic texts present the wise king as builder. Solomon’s wisdom is not merely intellectual but material, embodied in stone and cedar.
5: The queen is overwhelmed by the full display: the food, court officials, servants, their attire, the cupbearers, and the burnt offerings at the Temple. Wəlōʾ-hāyāh bāh ʿôd rûaḥ — “there was no longer any spirit in her.” The phrase describes breathless astonishment — a temporary loss of self-possession. The detail of “his burnt offerings which he offered at the house of the LORD” connects material grandeur to cultic fidelity: the Temple liturgy is part of what impresses her. The sacred and the political are not separated at Solomon’s court.
6: The queen’s verdict: “the report I heard in my own country about your deeds and your wisdom was true.” A Sabaean queen validates what Israel knew: YHWH’s gift of wisdom to Solomon is a verifiable fact accessible to outsiders. Her speech functions as a Gentile testimony — the nations confirming what the covenant people experience. Jesus cites her in this function (Matthew 12:42): “the queen of the South will rise up at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon.”
7: “The half was not told to me” (lōʾ huggad-lî haḥăṣî) — the reality exceeded the report. This rhetorical structure of wonder — every account falling short — governs the queen’s praise: yāsaptā ḥoḵmāh wāṭôḇ ʾel-haššəmuʿāh — you have exceeded in wisdom and prosperity the report I heard. The gap between report and reality is itself a form of divine self-disclosure: what can be said about YHWH’s gifts never fully captures them.
8: The queen pronounces a beatitude: ʾašrê ʾănāšêkā ʾašrê ʿăḇādêkā ʾēlleh hāʿōmḏîm lǝpānêkā tāmîd — “blessed are your men, blessed are these servants of yours who stand before you continually and hear your wisdom.” The ʾašrê (blessed/happy) formula is the opening of Psalm 1 and the beatitude form Jesus develops in the Sermon on the Mount. To stand before Solomon and hear his wisdom is, in her analysis, the happiest human condition. The Psalter uses the same formula for standing in YHWH’s presence (Psalms 84:4).
9: The queen blesses YHWH: yəhî yhwh ʾelōhêkā bārûk ʾăšer ḥāpēṣ bəkā — “blessed be the LORD your God, who has delighted in you.” A Sabaean queen praises YHWH, the God of Israel. The phrase “because the LORD loved Israel forever” — a Gentile queen articulates Israel’s covenant theology. This is the high-water mark of the Solomonic vision: the nations have come to Solomon, heard wisdom, and praised YHWH. The mission of 1 Kings 8:41-43 (that the foreigner may know YHWH’s name) is fulfilled in this moment.
10: The queen gives Solomon 120 talents of gold — the same amount Hiram sent (1 Kings 9:14), a precise parallel suggesting equivalent trading partners. Her spices were “very great” in quantity: bəśāmîm lārōḇ mǝʾōd — the spice haul exceeded all previous imports. The verse closes: “never again did there come such a quantity of spices as the queen of Sheba gave.” The finality implies this transaction was unique in Israelite history — a moment of trade, diplomacy, and wisdom-witness that would not recur.
11: Hiram’s Ophir fleet brought ʿăṣê ʾalmûggîm — almug wood (possibly red sandalwood from south Asia) — and precious stones. The almug wood was made into supports for the Temple and palace and into lyres and harps. The three-year round trip to Ophir (1 Kings 9:28) produced multiple luxury goods. The convergence of Hiram’s Ophir trade and the Sheba visit at this point in the narrative creates the impression of an empire at its commercial zenith.
12: The almug supports (misnᵊqôt) for the Temple and palace are a hapax detail — both structural and aesthetic, the exotic wood serving as architecture and decor. The same wood produced musical instruments for the court. The combination of Temple use and musical use connects the Sheba-and-Ophir material to the liturgical life of Jerusalem: even international trade flows toward YHWH’s house and its worship.
13: Solomon gave the queen “all she desired, whatever she asked,” plus gifts “according to the bounty of King Solomon.” The exchange is described as reciprocal but asymmetric: she came with maximum gifts, he outgave her. The narrative closes the Sheba episode with her departure. No political alliance is forged; no conversion is narrated. She came to hear wisdom, was convinced, praised YHWH, and returned. The episode stands complete as a testimony to wisdom’s international reach — and to the covenant community’s calling to be a light to the nations.
14: The annual gold income of 666 talents is a precise and theologically resonant figure. Revelation 13:18 uses the same number for the beast — “the number of a man.” Whether or not the Revelation author has Solomon in mind, the ancient reader of Kings would recognize the irony: the king whose wisdom was divine begins to accumulate at a rate that echoes the warning of Deuteronomy 17:17 — “he must not greatly multiply silver and gold for himself.” The excessive wealth is the beginning of the covert Deuteronomic critique that 1 Kings 11 will make explicit.
15: In addition to the 666 talents, revenue came from “traders and merchants, all the kings of Arabia, and the governors of the land.” The administrative term paḥôt (governors) reflects the provincial structure established in 1 Kings 4. The additional income streams mean the 666 figure is only part of a larger wealth flow. The empire’s commercial infrastructure generates revenue from multiple directions simultaneously, all flowing toward the king.
16: Solomon made 200 large shields (ṣinnôt) of hammered gold, each requiring 600 shekels of gold. The shields were decorative-ceremonial, not military — they adorned the House of the Forest of Lebanon (1 Kings 7:2). The use of gold for objects that would normally be functional signals the crossing into conspicuous excess: military symbols reduced to display. ANE rulers frequently made such display objects, but the Torah’s warning against excessive gold (Deuteronomy 17:17) is in the background.
17: Three hundred smaller shields (māginnôt) of hammered gold, three minas each. Both shield types were stored in the House of the Forest of Lebanon (v21), which functioned as an armory and treasury. The specific quantities and weights are recorded with the precision of an official inventory — the narrator presents these facts without comment, trusting the Deuteronomically literate reader to register the excess.
18: The ivory throne (kissēʾ šēn gādôl) overlaid with finest gold (mûpāz) represents the pinnacle of ANE royal furniture. Ivory thrones are attested archaeologically at Megiddo and at Ugarit and in the Nimrud ivories. The combination of ivory and gold was the luxury standard. The throne had six steps, a rounded top, and armrests with lion figures. The lion imagery (ʾăryôt) frames the throne with Judah’s tribal symbol (Genesis 49:9 — “Judah is a lion’s whelp”): the Davidic king enthroned as lion of his tribe.
19: The throne had six steps and a rounded top (the Hebrew raʾăšōt ʿăgulôt is disputed; LXX reads differently). The armrests were on either side of the seat; a lion stood beside each armrest. The architectural precision of the description matches the Temple building accounts. The detail that “nothing like it was ever made for any kingdom” is the narrator’s superlative — the throne of Solomon was without parallel in the ancient world.
20: Twelve lions stood on the six steps, one on each side of each step. The number twelve (tribes of Israel) and the symmetrical arrangement express the royal covenant: the king on the lion-throne presides over all Israel, supported by the symbolic completeness of the twelve-tribe structure. The throne is a political and theological architecture — its symbolism encodes the relationship between king, people, and covenant.
21: All Solomon’s drinking vessels were gold; the House of the Forest of Lebanon’s vessels were of pure gold. ʾÊn kāsep lōʾ neḥšāḇ bîmê šəlōmōh — “silver was not considered as anything in the days of Solomon.” This extreme statement marks the apex of wealth: when silver is worthless, gold has become commonplace. The Deuteronomic critique is implicit — the king was to be of “humble heart” (Deuteronomy 17:20), not one in whose reign precious metal was despised for abundance.
22: The “Tarshish fleet” (ʾŏnî tarśîš) — the designation probably refers to the class of large ocean-going vessels capable of long voyages rather than specifically vessels bound for Tarshish (likely Tartessus in Spain, though the term may be generic for the western Mediterranean). Once every three years they returned with gold, silver, ivory, apes (qôpîm), and peacocks (tukkîyîm — a disputed term; may mean baboons, parrots, or peacocks). The animals signal trade with sub-Saharan Africa or South Asia — the empire’s commercial reach extended beyond the Near East.
23: Wayyigdal hammelek šəlōmōh mikkol malkhê hāʾāreṣ lĕʾōšer ûlĕḥoḵmāh — “King Solomon surpassed all the kings of the earth in riches and wisdom.” This is the narrator’s summary statement, the rhetorical peak of the Solomonic narrative. The double superlative — greater in wealth AND wisdom than every other king — makes Solomon the embodiment of the ideal ruler of Psalm 72. The praise is real; the narrative does not retract it. But the structure of Kings places this summit immediately before the account of its dismantling in 1 Kings 11.
24: All the peoples of the earth sought audience with Solomon to hear the wisdom that God had placed in his heart (laʾăšer-śāmāh ʾelōhîm bəlibbô). The phrase “that God placed in his heart” keeps the wisdom explicitly divine in origin — not native Solomonic intellect but YHWH’s gift (cf. 1 Kings 3:12). The universal seeking of Solomon’s wisdom fulfills the Abrahamic promise that through Israel’s seed all nations would be blessed (Genesis 12:3).
25: Year after year every visitor brought a gift: silver, gold, garments, weapons, spices, horses, and mules. The annual tribute list covers luxury goods and military assets (horses, mules). The accumulation of horses from multiple nations quietly violates Deuteronomy 17:16 — “he must not acquire many horses for himself.” The narrator records the tribute list factually; the Deuteronomic critique operates through the reader’s knowledge of the law, not through editorial comment.
26: Solomon accumulated 1,400 chariots and 12,000 horsemen, stationed in chariot cities and in Jerusalem. The chariot cities — Megiddo, Hazor, Gezer (1 Kings 9:15-17) — have been archaeologically confirmed as sites of large stable complexes; Megiddo yielded stall foundations for hundreds of horses (scholars debate whether these are Solomonic or Ahab-era). The military buildup is presented as impressive; the Deuteronomic text (1 Kings 17:16) is not cited by the narrator but would be heard by any Torah-literate reader.
27: Wayyittēn hammelek ʾet-hakkesep bîrûšālayim kāʾăḇānîm — “the king made silver as common in Jerusalem as stones.” The simile is deliberately extreme — the rarest currency reduced to the commonest material. Cedar wood from Lebanon was equally commonplace. The rhetorical hyperbole serves two purposes: it glorifies the Solomonic era as unprecedented prosperity, and it sets up the implied judgment. The Torah warned that the king “must not greatly multiply silver and gold for himself lest his heart be lifted up” (Deuteronomy 17:17,20). Jerusalem has arrived at that condition.
28: Solomon’s horses were imported from Egypt (miṣrayim) and Kue (Cilicia in southeastern Asia Minor, a major horse-breeding region in antiquity). The royal traders (sōḥărê hammelek) purchased them at fixed prices. The Egyptian + Kue combination is precisely what Deuteronomy 17:16 prohibits: “he must not cause the people to return to Egypt to acquire many horses, since the LORD has said to you, ‘You shall never return that way again.’” The account of Solomon’s wealth in vv14-29 is a sustained portrait of Deuteronomic violation — gold, horses from Egypt, and (disclosed in 1 Kings 11) many wives — with no explicit condemnation, letting the Torah provide its own commentary.
29: The price of a chariot (600 shekels of silver) and horse (150 shekels) is recorded with commercial precision. The chariots were exported to “all the kings of the Hittites and the kings of Aram” through Solomon’s agency — Israel served as a military-trade middleman between Egypt and the northern states. The international commercial network is described with admiring detail. But the reader who knows Deuteronomy 17:16-17 has been presented with a systematic account of the king accumulating exactly what the law forbade: gold (v14), horses from Egypt (v28), and — as 1 Kings 11 will disclose — many wives (v1).
